Writing τ\tau candidate cutoff (weights τ\tau capped), method evaluates grid values τ\tau minimizes estimate mean squared error weighted total, MSE(τ)=bias(τ)2+var(τ),\text{MSE}(\tau) = \text{bias}(\tau)^2 + \text{var}(\tau), balancing bias introduced trimming τ\tau (related weight removed cutoff) variance contributed weights remain. chosen cutoff τ\tau smallest estimated MSE. two rules give different cutoffs: Tukey far-fence fixed, conservative rule, Potter searches cutoff minimizes estimated MSE — often aggressive, capping weights lowers overall error.

code: # benchmarks known BY REGION: a sex margin and an age-group margin per region. pop  <- transform(population,   age_grp = cut(age, c(0, 30, 45, 60, Inf), labels = c("18-30","31-45","46-60","60+"))) samp <- transform(sample_survey,   age_grp = cut(age, c(0, 30, 45, 60, Inf), labels = c("18-30","31-45","46-60","60+")))  sex_by_region <- as.data.frame(table(region = pop$region, sex     = pop$sex)) age_by_region <- as.data.frame(table(region = pop$region, age_grp = pop$age_grp))  dom <- weighting_spec(samp, base_weights = pw) |>   step_calibrate(method = "raking",                  totals = list(sex_by_region, age_by_region),                  count = "Freq", by = "region") |>   prep()  # within each region the weights reproduce BOTH margins w <- dom$final_weight round(xtabs(w ~ region + sex,     data = cbind(samp, w = w))) #>        sex #> region    F   M #>   North 825 745 #>   South 643 607 #>   East  467 460 #>   West  376 372 round(xtabs(w ~ region + age_grp, data = cbind(samp, w = w))) #>        age_grp #> region  18-30 31-45 46-60 60+ #>   North   415   499   408 248 #>   South   345   396   324 185 #>   East    252   309   232 134 #>   West    195   242   196 115 inc_by_region <- aggregate(income ~ region, population, sum)   # region, income resp <- subset(sample_survey, responded == 1)  lin_dom <- weighting_spec(resp, base_weights = pw) |>   step_calibrate(method = "linear", formula = ~ sex + income,                  totals = list(sex = sex_by_region, income = inc_by_region),                  count = "Freq", calfun = "raking", by = "region") |>   prep()  # the income total is reproduced within each region w   <- lin_dom$final_weight got <- tapply(w * resp$income, resp$region, sum) cbind(calibrated = round(got),       benchmark  = inc_by_region$income[match(names(got), inc_by_region$region)]) #>       calibrated benchmark #> North   36939535  36939535 #> South   22331582  22331581 #> East    13076958  13076958 #> West    14396933  14396933

Flexible engines nonresponse outcome models. Response propensities model-calibration outcomes can fitted logistic regression, CART, random forest gradient boosting (xgboost) — API, swap one argument. Cross-fitting tame overfitting. Flexible learners can overfit propensity blow weights; optional k-fold cross-fitting estimates unit --sample, folds formed cluster leakage. Calibration controls extreme weights. Beyond raking, post-stratification GREG, ridge (penalized) calibration relaxes targets keep weights stable many auxiliaries. Principled trimming. usual far-fence, plus Potter’s MSE-optimal cutoff, chosen data instead hand. Recipe-aware variance. bootstrap re-applies every step replicate, standard errors carry variability whole cascade. Development version. title: Declarative API for Staged Survey Weights
text: recipe inert: building computes nothing. prep() walks steps order estimates cascade factors; collect_weights() extracts final weights. Separating define apply makes whole process reproducible auditable, exactly lets bootstrap re-run entire cascade per replicate.
code: library(weightflow)  recipe <- weighting_spec(sample_one, base_weights = pw) |>   step_unknown_eligibility(unknown = unknown_elig, by = "region") |>   step_drop_ineligible(ineligible = ineligible) |>   # household nonresponse: the whole dwelling is lost (no roster), so the   # adjustment is at the household level and uses only frame information   step_nonresponse(respondent = hh_responded, method = "weighting_class",                    by = "region", cluster = "household_id") |>   step_select_within(prob = p_within) |>   # person nonresponse: among the selected persons, the roster gives sex and age   # even for those who did not respond, so a propensity model can use them   step_nonresponse(respondent = responded, method = "propensity",                    formula = ~ region + sex + age, engine = "logit",                    num_classes = 10) |>   step_calibrate(method = "raking",                  margins = list(region = c(table(population$region)),                                 sex    = c(table(population$sex)))) |>   step_trim_weights() |>   step_assert(max_deff = 3)  fitted <- prep(recipe)              # estimate the cascade summary(fitted)                     # per-stage diagnostics + Kish deff wts    <- collect_weights(fitted)   # data.frame with .weight

title: Bootstrap replicate weights that re-apply the recipe — bootstrap_weights

title: Calibration to population totals — step_calibrate
text: Adjusts weights weighted sample reproduces known population totals auxiliary variables, staying close possible input weights (Deville & Sarndal 1992). Supports raking (IPF categorical margins), post-stratification, linear/GREG calibration, optionally bounded (logit distance explicit bounds calibration factor). linear calibration, penalty enables ridge (penalized) calibration, relaxes targets control extreme weights many auxiliaries.

title: Calibration to population totals — step_calibrate
code: step_calibrate(   spec,   margins = NULL,   method = c("raking", "poststratify", "linear"),   formula = NULL,   totals = NULL,   count = NULL,   by = NULL,   cluster = NULL,   equal_within_cluster = FALSE,   calfun = c("linear", "logit", "raking"),   bounds = NULL,   maxit = 50L,   tol = 1e-06,   penalty = NULL )

title: Calibration to population totals — step_calibrate
text: spec weighting_spec. margins named list (classic format "raking"/"poststratify"). element named numeric vector target totals per category. E.g.: list(sex = c(M = 5000, F = 5200), region = c(N = 3000, S = 7200)). Still fully supported; tidy alternative see totals count. method "raking" (IPF, categorical margins), "poststratify" (post-strata: one categorical variables crossed) "linear" (GREG / regression estimator; handles continuous categorical auxiliaries together). formula (method = "linear") auxiliary formula, e.g. ~ sex + income. Uses model.matrix; includes intercept unless write ~ 0 + ... totals population totals, one two forms. Classic (methods): "linear" named numeric vector aligned model.matrix columns (including "(Intercept)" = N); "raking"/"poststratify" use margins. Tidy (recommended): data frame named list data frames/numbers giving totals friendly way, paired count. "poststratify", single data frame one category columns plus counts column. "raking", list data frames, one per margin. "linear", named list whose names match formula terms: data frame categories factor, single number continuous auxiliary; weightflow builds model.matrix totals internally (never handle intercept dropped reference category). count (tidy totals ) string naming counts column totals data frame(s). columns treated category variables. (tidy totals ) NULL, string naming domain (partition) column. given, weights calibrated independently within domain, totals (partitioned / domain calibration). totals tables carry domain column, count table split ; continuous total becomes data frame domain, value (one total per domain). domain variable must appear formula / margins: partition. Composes calfun, bounds, penalty equal_within_cluster, applied within domain. NULL (default) calibrates globally, . cluster (method = "linear") name cluster id column (e.g. "household"), equal weights within cluster. equal_within_cluster (method = "linear") logical. TRUE, Lemaitre-Dufour (1987) integrative calibration: single weight per cluster. Requires cluster. Final weights equal within cluster provided incoming weight also uniform within cluster. Works calfun distance ("linear", "raking" "logit"), bounds . calfun (method = "linear") distance function calibration factor g: "linear" (g = 1 + u, closed form), "raking" (g = exp(u), exponential/multiplicative distance, keeps weights positive still satisfies constraints exactly) "logit" (bounded construction; requires bounds). "raking" "logit" use iterative Deville-Sarndal solver work integrative option (equal_within_cluster) . bounds (method = "linear") numeric c(L, U) L < 1 < U. Bounds calibration factor g (g-weights). "linear" truncates; "logit" enforced smoothly. Avoids extreme/negative weights without separate trimming step. maxit, tol convergence control raking bounded calibration. penalty (method = "linear", unbounded) NULL positive cost(s) ridge (penalized) calibration. positive scalar applies cost every constraint; named vector sets cost per constraint (matched model.matrix columns). cost scale-free: large value keeps constraint (near) exact, small value relaxes control extreme weights many auxiliaries. ridge achieved totals longer match targets exactly; diagnostics report deviation.

title: Calibration to population totals — step_calibrate
code: # Raking to population margins weighting_spec(sample_survey, base_weights = pw) |>   step_nonresponse(respondent = responded, method = "weighting_class", by = "region") |>   step_calibrate(method = "raking",                  margins = list(sex    = c(table(population$sex)),                                 region = c(table(population$region)))) |>   prep() #>  #> == Weighting specification (weightflow) == #> Data    : 467 cases #> Base wts: pw #> Steps   : #>   1. nonresponse (weighting class) #>   2. calibration (raking) #> Status  : estimated (prep) #>  #> Stage summary: #>                     stage n_active sum_wts cv_wts deff_kish n_eff #>                      base      467    4371  0.236     1.056   442 #>  stage_1_step_nonresponse      270    4371  0.144     1.021   265 #>    stage_2_step_calibrate      270    4495  0.211     1.045   258 #>  #> deff_kish = 1 + CV^2 (Kish design effect from unequal weighting); #> n_eff = n_active / deff_kish. Both worsen with each adjustment and #> improve with trimming. #>   # ridge (penalized) calibration: relaxes the targets to control extreme # weights; a smaller penalty relaxes more. Uses only base R. pop_tot <- c("(Intercept)" = nrow(population),              regionSouth = sum(population$region == "South"),              regionEast  = sum(population$region == "East"),              regionWest  = sum(population$region == "West"),              sexM        = sum(population$sex == "M")) weighting_spec(sample_survey, base_weights = pw) |>   step_nonresponse(respondent = responded, method = "weighting_class", by = "region") |>   step_calibrate(method = "linear", formula = ~ region + sex,                  totals = pop_tot, penalty = 1) |>   prep() #>  #> == Weighting specification (weightflow) == #> Data    : 467 cases #> Base wts: pw #> Steps   : #>   1. nonresponse (weighting class) #>   2. calibration (linear, ridge) #> Status  : estimated (prep) #>  #> Stage summary: #>                     stage n_active sum_wts cv_wts deff_kish n_eff #>                      base      467    4371  0.236     1.056   442 #>  stage_1_step_nonresponse      270    4371  0.144     1.021   265 #>    stage_2_step_calibrate      270    4438  0.159     1.025   263 #>  #> deff_kish = 1 + CV^2 (Kish design effect from unequal weighting); #> n_eff = n_active / deff_kish. Both worsen with each adjustment and #> improve with trimming. #>   # --- Tidy `totals` format (recommended) --------------------------------- # Post-stratification: give the population counts as a data frame with one or # more category columns plus a counts column named by `count`. ps_totals <- as.data.frame(table(region = population$region, sex = population$sex)) weighting_spec(sample_survey, base_weights = pw) |>   step_calibrate(method = "poststratify", totals = ps_totals, count = "Freq") |>   prep() #>  #> == Weighting specification (weightflow) == #> Data    : 467 cases #> Base wts: pw #> Steps   : #>   1. calibration (poststratify) #> Status  : estimated (prep) #>  #> Stage summary: #>                   stage n_active sum_wts cv_wts deff_kish n_eff #>                    base      467    4371  0.236     1.056   442 #>  stage_1_step_calibrate      467    4495  0.306     1.093   427 #>  #> deff_kish = 1 + CV^2 (Kish design effect from unequal weighting); #> n_eff = n_active / deff_kish. Both worsen with each adjustment and #> improve with trimming. #>   # Raking: a list of data frames, one per margin. m_region <- as.data.frame(table(region = population$region)) m_sex    <- as.data.frame(table(sex = population$sex)) weighting_spec(sample_survey, base_weights = pw) |>   step_calibrate(method = "raking",                  totals = list(m_region, m_sex), count = "Freq") |>   prep() #>  #> == Weighting specification (weightflow) == #> Data    : 467 cases #> Base wts: pw #> Steps   : #>   1. calibration (raking) #> Status  : estimated (prep) #>  #> Stage summary: #>                   stage n_active sum_wts cv_wts deff_kish n_eff #>                    base      467    4371  0.236     1.056   442 #>  stage_1_step_calibrate      467    4495  0.295     1.087   430 #>  #> deff_kish = 1 + CV^2 (Kish design effect from unequal weighting); #> n_eff = n_active / deff_kish. Both worsen with each adjustment and #> improve with trimming. #>   # Linear/GREG with mixed auxiliaries: data frames for categoricals (all # categories) and a single number for a continuous total. weightflow builds # the model.matrix totals internally, so you never drop a reference category. resp <- subset(sample_survey, responded == 1) weighting_spec(resp, base_weights = pw) |>   step_calibrate(method = "linear", formula = ~ region + sex + income,                  totals = list(region = m_region, sex = m_sex,                                income = sum(population$income)),                  count = "Freq") |>   prep() #>  #> == Weighting specification (weightflow) == #> Data    : 270 cases #> Base wts: pw #> Steps   : #>   1. calibration (linear) #> Status  : estimated (prep) #>  #> Stage summary: #>                   stage n_active sum_wts cv_wts deff_kish n_eff #>                    base      270    2582  0.233     1.054   256 #>  stage_1_step_calibrate      270    4495  0.242     1.058   255 #>  #> deff_kish = 1 + CV^2 (Kish design effect from unequal weighting); #> n_eff = n_active / deff_kish. Both worsen with each adjustment and #> improve with trimming. #>   # Domain (partitioned) calibration: `by` calibrates independently within each # domain, each to its own totals. The domain is an extra column in the tidy # totals, not a term in the formula/margins. Here we rake two margins (sex and # age group) within each region, which a single global cross could not express. pop  <- transform(population,   age_grp = cut(age, c(0, 30, 45, 60, Inf), labels = c("18-30","31-45","46-60","60+"))) samp <- transform(sample_survey,   age_grp = cut(age, c(0, 30, 45, 60, Inf), labels = c("18-30","31-45","46-60","60+"))) sex_by_region <- as.data.frame(table(region = pop$region, sex     = pop$sex)) age_by_region <- as.data.frame(table(region = pop$region, age_grp = pop$age_grp)) weighting_spec(samp, base_weights = pw) |>   step_calibrate(method = "raking",                  totals = list(sex_by_region, age_by_region),                  count = "Freq", by = "region") |>   prep() #>  #> == Weighting specification (weightflow) == #> Data    : 467 cases #> Base wts: pw #> Steps   : #>   1. calibration (raking) #> Status  : estimated (prep) #>  #> Stage summary: #>                   stage n_active sum_wts cv_wts deff_kish n_eff #>                    base      467    4371  0.236     1.056   442 #>  stage_1_step_calibrate      467    4495  0.331     1.109   421 #>  #> deff_kish = 1 + CV^2 (Kish design effect from unequal weighting); #> n_eff = n_active / deff_kish. Both worsen with each adjustment and #> improve with trimming. #>

NULL, cap chosen automatically method. method rule automatic cap upper = NULL: "tukey" (default, Q3 + 3*IQR far-fence) "potter" (Potter's MSE-optimal cutoff, grid candidate cutoffs minimizes estimate bias^2 + variance balances bias trimming variance extreme weights). Ignored upper supplied. strict logical. TRUE (default), iterate cap+redistribution weight outside [lower, upper] (like survey's strict = TRUE). FALSE, single pass (redistribution may push weights slightly past cap). maxit integer. Maximum iterations strict = TRUE.

title: weightflow 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-06-30 First release. dependency-free, pipeable API compute survey weights design base weights chain hierarchical adjustment stages. Build recipe lazily, estimate prep(), extract weights collect_weights(). Separating define apply makes whole process reproducible auditable, lets bootstrap re-run entire cascade replicate.

title: weightflow 0.1.0
text: step_unknown_eligibility() — redistribute weight unknown-eligibility cases known ones (person- household-level via cluster). step_drop_ineligible() — zero --scope units. step_select_within() — within-household selection (unequal prob equal n_eligible). step_nonresponse() — weighting-class propensity adjustment, person household level (cluster). step_calibrate() — raking, post-stratification linear/GREG calibration, bounded (Deville-Särndal) integrative (one weight per household) cluster options. step_model_calibration() — Wu-Sitter model calibration. step_trim(), step_trim_weights(), step_round(), step_rescale() — trimming, rounding rescaling. step_assert() — quality checkpoint (deff, weight ratio, effective n).

title: weightflow 0.1.0
text: summary(), plot() weight_factors() per-stage diagnostics. design_effect() Kish design effect effective sample size. report_weighting() builds self-contained HTML report pipeline diagram, variables used, per-stage summaries per-step visuals.

title: weightflow 0.1.0
text: following available development version GitHub planned future CRAN release: Post-stratification: data frame one category columns plus counts column. Several category columns crossed automatically, need build collapsed cell variable hand. Raking: list data frames, one per margin. Linear/GREG: named list matching formula terms, data frame (categories) factor single number continuous total; weightflow builds model.matrix totals internally, user never drops reference category handles intercept. Calibration also reports clearer diagnostics warnings: post-stratification flags cells sample missing totals (error) totals absent sample (warning); raking warns mutually inconsistent margins non-convergence; linear calibration warns constraints fully satisfied; calibration variables missing values raise informative error. Subsampling one person per household step_select_within(). new n_selected argument (single number unquoted column) works alongside n_eligible simple random selection subsample: weight multiplied n_eligible / n_selected (equivalent prob = n_selected/n_eligible). defaults 1, selecting single person keeps working unchanged. External consistency totals step_model_calibration(). totals x_formula auxiliaries can now supplied new x_totals argument, two shapes step_calibrate(method = "linear"): tidy format (named list data frame per factor, paired count, single number per continuous total) classic model-matrix vector. covers common case X control totals come external source rather frame; auxiliaries need present sample, population. x_totals NULL (default) X totals still taken population, existing code unchanged. population remains required, model-assisted block predicts outcome every population unit. Model calibration now also warns, like linear calibration, achieved totals fully satisfy constraints (collinear ill-conditioned auxiliaries). Delete--PSU jackknife variance (recipe-aware). jackknife_weights() builds jackknife replicate weights deleting one PSU time re-running whole recipe replicate, replicate weights carry variability every adjustment. stratified jackknife (JKn) strata/psu, unstratified jackknife (JK1) strata = NULL, delete-one-unit jackknife psu = NULL. jackknife_estimate() (plus jack_total() / jack_mean()) summarise statistic JKn variance match survey’s replicate jackknife totals. as_svrepdesign() now also accepts jackknife object, recipe-aware replicate weights flow survey/srvyr estimand domain. Domain (partitioned) calibration step_calibrate(). new argument names domain (partition) column; weights calibrated independently within domain, totals (partitioned / domain calibration). tidy totals carry domain column, continuous total becomes data frame domain, value (one total per domain); domain variable go formula/margins. composes calfun, bounds, penalty equal_within_cluster, applied within domain, reproduces every domain’s benchmarks. = NULL (default) calibrates globally, unchanged. Exponential (raking) distance step_calibrate(method = "linear"). calfun now also accepts "raking" (multiplicative distance g = exp(u)), next "linear" "logit". keeps calibration weights positive without needing explicit bounds still satisfies constraints exactly, works mixed categorical continuous auxiliaries well integrative option (equal_within_cluster, one weight per cluster). Matches survey::calibrate(calfun = "raking"). R-indicator response representativity (automatic diagnostic). recipe includes nonresponse adjustment, summary() report_weighting() now report R-indicator (Schouten, Cobben & Bethlehem), R = 1 - 2*S, S design-weighted standard deviation estimated response propensities eligible sample: closer 1 means representative response less nonresponse-bias risk. report also shows unconditional partial R-indicators auxiliary, pointing variable drives lack representativity. computed auxiliaries nonresponse step needs new function user action; recipes without nonresponse step unaffected. New disposition column sample_one example data. single factor full field disposition (eligible respondent, eligible nonrespondent, household nonresponse, ineligible, unknown eligibility), recoded existing indicator columns (kept). gives tidy single-column view dispositions can used directly via logical conditions steps. New vignette “Preparing sample: eligibility response weighting”, input sample classified (disposition tree), sized (eligibility response inflation) dispositions map adjustment steps. Machine-learning response propensities (CART, random forest gradient boosting via xgboost) step_nonresponse() step_model_calibration(). k-fold cross-fitting (crossfit) estimate unit --sample, folds formed cluster avoid leakage. Ridge (penalized) calibration (penalty) keep weights stable many auxiliaries. Potter MSE-optimal trimming (method = "potter"), data-driven cutoff. Install remotes::install_github("jpferreira33/weightflow") use today.
